It's a 9-mile long, 3 1/2-mile wide mountain of sand, 76 miles out to sea from
Harbor, that boasts a fascinating history highlighted by the tale of
, one of the most astounding true stor[i]es of one person's fight for survival ever recorded in the annals of time.
brought the curtain down on the centuries-old Channel Island Indian culture that thrived through five distinct civilizations.

is a bird Island, populated by thousands of ravens, cormorants, sea gulls, blue herons, bald eagles, canaries, larks and robins. It's also a rocket base for solar studies by International Geophysical Year scientists.
, the saint's day celebrated December 6, 1602, when the Spanish explorer's ship sailed by
Some 68 prehistoric Indian village sites have been identified on
who estimate that well over 1,000 Indians lived on
, a more peaceful people than those who dwelled on
, as evidenced by the scarcity of arrowheads unearthed, existed on a seafood and bird economy, deriving their food supply from abalone, fish, sea lions, sea elephants and birds.
apparently never supported a great deal of plant life in the time of man's existence on it.

returned to the Island camp flushed with excitement. He told of seeing a figure running in the distance. He said he tried to catch up with the Island phantom, but failed. Truly, it did not seem possible. Could it be
Several trips and three years later—on another otter hunting excursion to

, spotted a basket containing a robe sewed of birds' plumage. Now they were convinced. Incredible as it seemed, they knew
was still there and very much alive.
abandoned his hunt for otter. He ordered
, canyon by canyon, cave by cave, never quitting until
who made the discovery a few days later. He traced her footprints to a grass shelter on one of the Island peaks.
She was seated in her hideaway, observing members of
searching the beach below for her. A pack of dogs, trained to answer the wild woman's instant command, was at her side. Two pet ravens were also in the roofless enclosure.
, she accepted his presence calmly, ordering the dogs away. Despite the fact that
was the first person she faced in 18 long difficult years, she did not become alarmed.
was dressed in cormorant feathers, a garment identical to the one found earlier by
. She was in excellent physical condition. There was a large supply of food in her grass hut.
, she told them the story of her 18-year existence on
, not by word of mouth, for none could understand her dialect. But through sign language, she explained how she hid when boats came to
was found dead when she swam to

, had walked its length and breadth through the years with her dogs ever at her side. She never wanted for food, living off birds, fish, abalone and seals' blubber. Water was easy to obtain from the many existing springs. Her life was lonely. But in time she came to accept it and fled in fear from every visitor.
cared for her. The story of her discovery spread afar.
The curious came from miles to see
, who, in reality, was a quiet, shy person amazingly attractive despite her unique ordeal.
searched without success for one of
in 1835. But no one could be found who spoke the woman's language.
Civilization proved to be a greater challenge than life alone on
. Despite her new diet of proper food, or perhaps because of it, she failed rapidly. In a short time she became too weak to walk. Within three months of her discovery, the last of the
With her also died the great culture of
On her death bed one of the priests from the
baptized her, giving her the name Juana Maria. Her robe of cormorant feathers was sent to
, where it now rests in the
, where a plaque erected to her memory in 1928 reads:

believes he has the skeleton of the
for 30 years collecting Indian artifacts for the
He is convinced his skeleton, one of thousands of Indian relics he has collected and catalogued from
One reason for his conviction is that the skeleton was buried deeper than most on
, which is being blown to sea by cyclone-like storms that buffet
continuously. Another item that supports his belief is that beads buried with
were imported there in the 19th century. Sanger's Indian collection includes many skeletons, and novel whale and sea turtle effigies, apparently used by the Indians as good luck pieces.
introduced sheep to the already badly eroded
. He and a continual succession ofIsland lessees sowed salt grass on the sandy terrain to provide nourishment for the thousands of sheep that roamed its hills and canyons for 84 years.
. The latter ended this phase of Island activity in 1941 with the activation of
during World War II, first as an Army base and later as a Navy station.
maintained a weather station there. Total population of
as a school teacher for a time. Her student body consisted of
was known as the smallest school in
boasting a full time, fully accredited school teacher.
has served as one of the nation's most important guided missile proving grounds. In fact,
, was established solely because of the presence of
In the past 10 years a $5,000,000 labyrinth of electronic networks has been scattered about the sand-encrusted Island hills of
. Theodolite, telemeter and radar towers speckle Island peaks, knolls and sand-swept mesas.
provides missile scientists with a perfect testing range. Instruments at
, at the Santa Cruz Island facility and at
record data which enable scientists and engineers to evaluate operations of the various missiles in flight.
Some missiles are fired into the sea between
. Others such as the Regulus are actually put through their paces, guided to and landed on the San Nicolas airstrip and then shipped back by boat to
From the moment a guided missile is placed into its launcher until it is landed, destroyed or dragged dripping from
, it is the subject of at least one and often as many as 40 cameras. These photographs provide scientists with proof positive why the weapon did or did not react as anticipated.
The 250 Navy and civilian technicians stationed on bleak
are all vitally involved in the study and development of guided missiles.
," declared the officer in charge of the
, as we disembarked from one of the two Navy four-engine Douglas DC4s that make daily runs between
Swarms of sea gulls and raucous ravens soared above and about
operations tower, which also serves as administrative headquarters for the San Nicolas Island base.
A gust of wind ripped across the airstrip and several giant ravens perched on wires by the operations tower were blown upside down and were having a time righting themselves.
Jim, the station's pet raven, buzzed us as we walked toward operations, and then gave chase to the Island's three dogs, Cecil, Rusty and Oscar, all males.
"Women are verboten here. That holds true for the dogs, and Sam, our pet cat. This is strictly a man's island," exclaimed the operations officer.
"And a haven for ravens," he added, as Jim landed at our feet, tugged at the officer's pants cuffs and then nipped him playfully on the leg.
There are thousands of the big, black birds on
, descendants of the same birds considered sacred by
. Ravens exist on mice that also thrive on
"Jim hurt his leg more than a year ago," we were told, "and some of the fellows at operations took him to sick bay and patched up the injury. Now he's everybody's friend."
After being assigned quarters in one of the Island's concrete barracks, we rejoined the operations officer at Operations on the 502-foot airstrip.
, ringed by cliffs averaging 500 feet in height, rises to a peak elevation of 890 feet.
We climbed aboard a powerful wagon for a trip to

, an amazing stretch of sculptured rocks that extends to a needle-nose point into the sea. Here, abalone and lobster abound. Station personnel need not be skin divers to pry pink, blue and black abalone by the hundreds off weird shaped, eroded, sponge-like rocks during low tide. All that is necessary is to walk out on the rocks and pull them off. You don't even get your feet wet ...
At night San Nicolas missile men drive to
and catch lobster with bare hands. It's a great Island sport. Saucer-like pockets of clear water nestled in rocks at

were filled with gorgeous ocean life—deep purple sea grass, sea urchins, brown sea moss, sea lettuce, black snails, hermit crabs and sea anemones of various sizes and colors. A 15-foot, jet black whale, victim of a vicious sea battle, washed ashore a day or two before our arrival, lay on a nearby sand spit. The operations officer checked his lobster traps and shouted with glee as he removed a half dozen tempting red snappers.
Although unauthorized personnel are not allowed to land on

amphibian line flies chartered groups to waters off the Island coast. This is Probert's skin divers' special. He anchors his amphib for four hours while sportsmen dive from the plane exploring the fascinating sea depths and bring up abalone and lobster for the flight home.
we drove across five miles of sandy waste, by-passing evidences of kitchen middens and innumerable fossilized tree stumps, indication of the presence at one time of small forests on the now barren
. Shrapnel, 20 mm. shells and live 100-pound bombs littered a large section of sand hills—souvenirs of the Thirties and Forties when
was used as a Navy firing range.
At one point we paused on the sandy roadbed to descend to a half-hidden
where an Indian artist had scrawled drawings of ocean waves and sharks in various forms of motion. We wondered if
had lived in this cave, if perhaps it was she who made the lasting impressions on the walls.

A short distance from the cave a tremendous thundering discordant symphony suddenly rent the air. We had arrived at the beginning of the five-mile stretch of sea lion beaches, inhabited by more than 5,000 of the peculiar pinnipeds.

As we walked gingerly toward the sea lions, perched on rocks above the breaking surf, to get close-up pictures, the playful pinnipeds became aware of our presence and stirred up a storm—howling, barking, sneezing, yawning, squealing as they wriggled to the edge of the rocks and flipped into the water.
"Go away!" they seemed to be shouting as they splashed about in the sea.
has the largest number of sea lions found anywhere in the nation and one of the few herds of sea elephants in the world. More than 300 sea elephants, cows and their offspring have been counted on
. The ugly mammal gets its name from its proboscis, a grotesque trunklike snout more than a foot long.

The sea elephants, sea cows and baby sea elephants put on an even more hilarious show than did the sea lions. Too lazy to care, these tremendous sad-eyed wrinkled beasts hardly moved as we walked about their herd. Except for the king bull.
In the colony we visited there were about 50 mouse-faced cows, each seven to 10 feet long and weighing more than a ton. There were almost as many babies in the colony as cows.
A short distance from the herd were a half-dozen bulls lying on scattered rocks. Whenever one of the bulls outside the herd would try to move in near a cow, a battle royal began.

The king bull flopped over cows and rocks to the interloper, making the distance in jig time considering his size, 12 to 18 feet, and weighing in excess of 2 1/2 tons. Ripping into the invader with his tusk-like teeth, the king bull in each instance of battle swiftly drew blood and chased off his challenger.
During repeated battles, cows and babies yapped, screamed, bellowed and barked, constantly curling their flippers that resemble giant-sized hands across their backs and scratching their skin.
It was equally entertaining to observe the cooperation among the various wildlife on
—cooperation, that is, in some instances and downright thievery in others.
Cormorants, sea lions, sea elephants and sea gulls sounded alarms for each other, with the arrival of indigenous Island fox or wild cats that roam

in large numbers. They also "blew the whistle" as we approached one beach after another. The sea gulls took to the air. This alerted the cormorants. And when they swarmed into the air the rush of their wings alarmed the sea lions and sea elephants who subsequently took to the water.
However, at times when cormorants took flight, sea gulls rushed to their nests and promptly chomped away on cormorant eggs, while ravens invaded sea gull nests and devoured sea gull eggs.
We also saw ravens ground an Island eagle as they protected their centuries-old reign as sacred rulers of their Island domain.
Sea elephants and cows were a major source of oil during old whaling days and were almost hunted to extinction. They are now making a comeback and increasing in numbers from year to year at
. Taking them in captivity is prohibited in United States waters.
Later, back at the base, we sat in on a yarn-swapping session between
, area supervisor of range instrumentation, came to
in 1946 when the missile base was activated. He has lived on
longer than anyone else now there.
San Nicolas' highest hill has been named
is the veteran Islander among Navy men. They were discussing the winds that continuously sweep
"Our prevailing northwest winds average between 35 and 50 m.p.h. nearly every day of the year,"
"But it blows much faster than that several times during the week,"
added. "We have no half-way weather out her[e]. Sandstorms can be expected at all times. Fine sand seeps into everything and plays havoc with our machinery."
"Everybody learns to lean into the winds,"
continued. "When we go ashore, we can always spot someone from
by the way he walks—with a San Nicolas tilt."
"The wind blows so fierce out here,"
noted, "that we'll build a road with a bulldozer on Friday and by Monday there won't be a sign of the road left."
There are 10 miles of paved road on
has its own fresh water supply. Tapped from Thousand Springs in the heart of
, it has a mineral taste, but those who live on
swear by it, favoring it over mainland drinking water.
, like most of the personnel on

, enjoy hiking and fishing. They're both Island authorities and have unearthed four complete skeletons. Noteworthy feature of the skeletons are the teeth—none missing and not a pinpoint of cavity. But the teeth, in each case, were worn down by the rigorous chewing habits of their owners.
is baseball—when the winds aren't blowing.
"This place grows on you," one of the men declared. "We get ashore regularly, if we want. And we can save money over here. No one can say that
with its crazy ravens, sea elephants and good fishing is a dull Island. And, of course, the missiles keep us busy."
is playing an important role. At the dawn of the IGY year a mighty Deacon rocket with a Nike booster roared skyward from

as a team of scientists began a study of solar rays in their relation to radio waves. The rocket was fired to penetrate the ionosphere, 50 miles above the earth, where radio waves are reflected back to earth. However, during solar flareups instead of bouncing the radio waves back to the earth, the ionosphere absorbs them, causing a radio blackout.

The rocket flights measure ultraviolet light and X-rays during solar flares. Valuable information about the explosions on the sun is being recorded at the IGY station. Methods too are being learned for forecasting radio blackouts.
which crashed and sank on the 15-foot high rock that rises abruptly from a depth of 50 fathoms. The
ran head-on into the tiny pyramid of guano-capped land in 1824.
A reef extends northward and southward of the rock a little over 100 yards in each direction. A lighted whistle buoy marks
500 yards northward of its peak. The
has several lighted floats each about 10 feet long and four feet wide in its vicinity.
is complete either without a buried treasure story. For there are legends galore about buried treasures here as well as on almost every one of the other
This story is but a year old. Two of the Navy men found an old weathered parchment which captured the imagination of mainlanders from
and all points in between. The parchment, barely legible, was discovered beneath the floor boards of an abandoned sheepherder's cabin. It read:

At Christmas time when gifts are sent They're from the saint, a kind old gent. On an isle which bears his name Is treasure, hid for you to claim In an iron chest so strong Buried where the land is long. If you walk where the tides do meet You will find it at your feet. Coins so rare, coins so old Coins of silver and of gold. If he who finds it is my friend, All my fortune he may spend. If he who finds it is my foe, Curses, misery and woe.
If the person who composed the ditty actually knew of a buried treasure, then it is or was at
